title: A genome-scale CRISPRi perturbation atlas of human induced pluripotent stem cells - Nature Biotechnology
gist: A group led by Prashant Mali (UC San Diego) built a genome-scale CRISPRi Perturb-Seq atlas of human iPSCs (11,692 genes, >2.5M single cells in the KOLF2.1J reference line), reconstructing the pluripotency 'cell map' from transcriptional correlations and using it to discover new regulators (ZBTB41, RNF7, DBR1) plus a transcriptome-wide RNA-editing screen.

insights.claim: A genome-scale CRISPRi Perturb-Seq atlas was generated in KOLF2.1J human induced pluripotent stem cells, mapping transcriptional phenotypes for 11,692 perturbed genes across more than 2.5 million single cells.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Sets a new reference-scale functional-genomics dataset for the pluripotency field; the throughput bar (millions of cells, whole expressed genome) is what late-stage single-cell CRISPR platforms and their tooling vendors now must match.
insights.quote: we generated an expressed genome-scale CRISPRi perturbation cell atlas in KOLF2.1J human induced pluripotent stem cells, mapping transcriptional phenotypes associated with 11,692 perturbed genes across >2.5 million single cells

insights.claim: The full perturbation atlas is released openly with FASTQ files under BioProject PRJNA1173491, an aggregated anndata matrix on Figshare, and a public GitHub web browser, rather than kept as proprietary IP.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Open release lowers the moat around the raw data but seeds an ecosystem; value accrues to companies building models and discovery pipelines on top rather than to data ownership itself.

insights.claim: The atlas was built on the KOLF2.1J line, positioned as a community reference human iPSC line for large-scale collaborative studies, standardizing the genetic background across perturbation experiments.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Convergence on a single reference line makes datasets cross-comparable and compoundable, favoring platforms and reagents standardized to KOLF2.1J.

insights.claim: The team built a dedicated analysis pipeline named PASTA (Perturbation Atlas Sanitization, Triage, and Analysis) that sanitizes cells, triages ineffective perturbations, and defines 'Strong Perturbations' passing all filters before mapping.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: The reusable triage framework (energy distance + DEG count filtering) is a template for extracting signal from noisy pooled single-cell screens, a persistent bottleneck for the whole Perturb-Seq category.
insights.locator: Extended Data Fig. 2 Data Analysis Pipeline PASTA

insights.claim: Perturbations were retained only if they had at least 25 associated cells and achieved greater than 30% on-target knockdown, filtering weak guides before downstream analysis.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Concrete QC thresholds set expectations for reproducibility; screens that ignore per-perturbation coverage/knockdown depth generate misleading hits.

insights.claim: In the pilot screens 92% of perturbations had more than 25 associated cells and 65% demonstrated on-target repression greater than 30%, quantifying the yield of a well-run CRISPRi single-cell screen.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Benchmarks the effective hit rate of the platform; roughly a third of perturbations fail the knockdown bar even in a tuned system, framing realistic expectations for CRISPRi coverage.

insights.claim: The pipeline uses an Isolation Forest to filter spurious non-targeting-control cells after Harmony batch alignment, assuming a 30% contamination fraction among controls.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Signals that even negative controls carry large noise fractions in single-cell screens; robust anomaly detection is now table stakes for credible hit calling.

insights.claim: The KRABZIM3-dCas9 CRISPRi effector was used for gene repression in KOLF2.1J cells, favoring interference (knockdown) over knockout to keep essential-gene phenotypes measurable.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: CRISPRi's tunable partial repression is what lets a screen assay essential and pluripotency-critical genes without simply killing cells, a design edge over CRISPR knockout in stem cells.

insights.claim: Correlations among perturbed transcriptional phenotypes were sufficient to reconstruct known protein complexes and cellular processes, with clusters mapping onto CORUM complexes and STRING interaction networks.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that functional relationships (who works with whom) can be read out purely from perturbation-response similarity, validating Perturb-Seq as a de novo interactome-discovery tool.
insights.quote: demonstrating rich recapitulation of functionally related protein complexes

insights.claim: The atlas independently recovered the Ragulator/LAMTOR complex and mitochondrial translation machinery as perturbation clusters, then confirmed the physical interactions using orthogonal SEC-MS proteomics on wild-type KOLF2.1J cells.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Cross-validation between transcriptional-phenotype clustering and mass-spec fractionation is the kind of orthogonal confirmation that de-risks screen-derived hypotheses for downstream target work.

insights.claim: The screen recapitulated the known mechanism by which UHRF1 recruits DNMT1 to maintain DNA methylation at hemimethylated sites, with DNMT1 and UHRF1 knockdowns producing overlapping transcriptional phenotypes.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Recovering textbook epigenetic partnerships from unbiased data builds confidence that the atlas's novel co-clusters reflect real biology, not artifacts.

themes.theme: Fitness and essential-gene landscape of pluripotency

insights.claim: A CRISPRi fitness screen measured perturbation effects at day 6 and day 14, identifying both fitness-enriching perturbations (Z>1) and essential genes (Z<-1 moderate, Z<-2 strong) specific to the pluripotent state.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A pluripotency-specific dependency map complements cancer DepMap; genes essential only in the stem state are candidate levers for controlling iPSC self-renewal and safety.

insights.claim: The KOLF2.1J essential-gene set was cross-referenced against the cancer DepMap, separating genes essential across cancers from those uniquely essential in the pluripotent context.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Distinguishing pluripotency-specific from pan-lethal dependencies helps target iPSC biology without hitting the same nodes cancer therapeutics already crowd around.

insights.claim: Gene-level day-14 fitness Z-scores correlated with an independent KOLF2 iPSC CRISPRi dataset from Usluer et al., with strong essential genes overlapping across the two studies.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Reproducibility across independent labs on the same line strengthens the atlas as a dependable reference rather than a one-off result.

themes.theme: Novel regulator discoveries: ZBTB41 and RNF7

insights.claim: The atlas surfaced ZBTB41 as a previously uncharacterized metabolic factor, validated through metabolic tracing, immunofluorescence and protein-protein interaction assays.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A new metabolic node in pluripotency is a fresh target hypothesis for controlling stem-cell state and a candidate IP seed for the founders' companies.
insights.quote: we then explored the atlas to uncover metabolic factor ZBTB41 and pluripotency regulator RNF7

insights.claim: RNF7 (also known as SAG/RBX2), an antioxidant protein and CUL5-associated E3 ubiquitin ligase component, was validated as a pluripotency regulator whose knockdown drives cells toward differentiation.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Links redox/ubiquitin-ligase biology to exit from pluripotency, a mechanistically actionable axis for differentiation control and oxidative-stress management in cell manufacturing.

insights.claim: RNF7 knockdown produced transcriptional profiles overlapping with POU5F1 (OCT4) knockdown, both enriching a 'Primitive Endoderm' signature and depleting an 'Epiblast'/pluripotency signature by GSEA.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Positions RNF7 functionally alongside the master pluripotency factor OCT4, elevating it from obscure ligase to a candidate master-regulator-adjacent target.

insights.claim: BioID proximity-labeling mapped the RNF7 interactome (210 enriched preys) and POU5F1 interactome (164 enriched preys), recovering known RNF7 partners CUL5/UBE2F and OCT4 partners including SALL4, ARID1B and SWI/SNF and MLL3/4 complex subunits.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Detailed interactomes convert screen hits into druggable complex maps, the raw material for degrader and protein-interaction-modulator programs.

insights.claim: The team ran a genome-scale screen of A-to-I RNA-editing modulators assayed by direct transcriptome-wide RNA-editing readout, going beyond reporter-based proxies.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Direct transcriptome-wide editing readout is a more faithful assay for the ADAR/RNA-editing therapeutic space, relevant to companies developing programmable RNA editing.
insights.quote: we leveraged the atlas to generate a genome-scale screen of A-to-I RNA-editing modulators assayed through direct transcriptome-wide RNA editing

insights.claim: The debranching enzyme DBR1 was uncovered and mechanistically validated as a potent regulator of A-to-I RNA editing, linking lariat/intron recycling to editing activity.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A new upstream lever on RNA-editing levels; modulating DBR1 could tune ADAR substrate availability, a novel angle for editing-based therapeutics and interferon-pathway control.
insights.quote: uncovering and mechanistically validating DBR1 as a potent regulator

insights.claim: Editing analysis compared ADAR and DBR1 knockdowns at a strict significance threshold (q<0.01), quantifying sites with increased versus decreased editing to establish DBR1's directional effect.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Directional, site-resolved editing effects distinguish genuine editing modulators from indirect ADAR-expression effects, refining target credibility.

insights.claim: The authors framed DBR1's role via intronic endogenous double-stranded RNA and antiviral-response biology, connecting lariat/intron recycling defects to innate immune dsRNA sensing.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: The dsRNA/interferon linkage ties RNA-editing modulation to immune-oncology and autoinflammatory mechanisms, broadening the therapeutic surface beyond stem-cell biology.

insights.claim: A directed regulatory graph was built where each Strong Perturbation is a node connected to its differentially expressed genes, and PageRank on the reverse graph identified the top 50 master regulators of the pluripotent network.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Graph-centrality ranking of regulators is a reusable way to prioritize the highest-leverage targets from perturbation data, useful for target-selection pipelines.

insights.claim: Corresponding author Prashant Mali is a scientific cofounder of Shape Therapeutics, Boundless Biosciences, Navega Therapeutics, Pi Bio and Engine Biosciences, tying the atlas's RNA-editing and target-discovery findings to a cluster of commercial ventures.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: The RNA-editing (Shape Therapeutics) and target-discovery angles map directly onto founder-linked companies; watch for these findings feeding private pipelines.

insights.claim: The work is framed as a step toward a foundation model of causal cell and tissue biology built on a Perturbation Cell and Tissue Atlas, aligning single-cell perturbation data with AI model-building ambitions.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Positions large perturbation atlases as training substrate for causal cell-biology foundation models, the emerging 'AI for biology' data-moat thesis backed by CZ Biohub and similar players.
